title: Set up Terraform for AWS infrastructure as code
summary: Initialize a Terraform project for AWS — VPC, EC2, RDS, S3 bucket, remote state in S3+DynamoDB, and a GitHub Actions workflow for plan/apply.
declaration: I will set up a Terraform project to provision AWS infrastructure including VPC networking, EC2 compute, RDS database, and S3 storage. Remote state will be stored in S3 with DynamoDB locking. I will also add a GitHub Actions workflow for automated plan/apply. If you need GCP or Azure instead, say so before I begin.
instructions:
## Set up Terraform for AWS infrastructure as code

### 1. Pre-flight checks
- Check if Terraform is installed: `terraform -version`. Target Terraform >= 1.6.
- If not installed on macOS: `brew tap hashicorp/tap && brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform`
- If not installed on Linux: download from `https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/`
- Check if AWS CLI is installed and configured: `aws sts get-caller-identity`. If not configured: `aws configure`
- Ensure you have an AWS IAM user or role with sufficient permissions (or use AWS SSO).
- Check if an S3 bucket for Terraform state already exists. If not, create one (step 3).

### 2. Project structure
- Create a `terraform/` directory in your project root:
```
terraform/
  main.tf          # Root module: calls sub-modules or defines top-level resources
  variables.tf     # Input variable declarations
  outputs.tf       # Output value declarations
  providers.tf     # AWS provider + required_providers block
  backend.tf       # Remote state backend config
  terraform.tfvars # Variable values (DO NOT commit if it contains secrets)
  modules/
    vpc/           # VPC, subnets, route tables, IGW
    app/           # EC2, security groups, IAM role
    database/      # RDS or Aurora
    storage/       # S3 buckets
```
- This modular layout keeps each concern isolated and reusable.

### 3. Bootstrap: create the S3 state bucket and DynamoDB lock table
- Terraform state must exist before Terraform can manage itself. Bootstrap manually:
```bash
# Create the S3 bucket for state (pick a globally unique name)
aws s3api create-bucket \
  --bucket my-project-terraform-state \
  --region us-east-1

# Enable versioning so you can roll back state
aws s3api put-bucket-versioning \
  --bucket my-project-terraform-state \
  --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled

# Block all public access
aws s3api put-public-access-block \
  --bucket my-project-terraform-state \
  --public-access-block-configuration \
    BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

# Enable server-side encryption
aws s3api put-bucket-encryption \
  --bucket my-project-terraform-state \
  --server-side-encryption-configuration '{"Rules":[{"ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault":{"SSEAlgorithm":"AES256"}}]}'

# Create DynamoDB table for state locking
aws dynamodb create-table \
  --table-name my-project-terraform-locks \
  --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \
  --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \
  --billing-mode PAY_PER_REQUEST
```
- The DynamoDB table prevents two `terraform apply` runs from corrupting state simultaneously.

### 4. Configure providers and backend

#### `terraform/providers.tf`
```hcl
terraform {
  required_version = ">= 1.6"

  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "aws" {
  region = var.aws_region

  default_tags {
    tags = {
      Project     = var.project_name
      Environment = var.environment
      ManagedBy   = "terraform"
    }
  }
}
```

#### `terraform/backend.tf`
```hcl
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket         = "my-project-terraform-state"
    key            = "prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region         = "us-east-1"
    dynamodb_table = "my-project-terraform-locks"
    encrypt        = true
  }
}
```
- Use different `key` paths for different environments: `prod/terraform.tfstate`, `staging/terraform.tfstate`.

### 5. Define variables

#### `terraform/variables.tf`
```hcl
variable "aws_region" {
  description = "AWS region to deploy resources"
  type        = string
  default     = "us-east-1"
}

variable "project_name" {
  description = "Project name used for resource naming and tags"
  type        = string
}

variable "environment" {
  description = "Deployment environment (prod, staging, dev)"
  type        = string
  default     = "prod"
}

variable "db_password" {
  description = "RDS master password — provide via TF_VAR_db_password env var"
  type        = string
  sensitive   = true
}
```

#### `terraform/terraform.tfvars`
```hcl
project_name = "my-project"
environment  = "prod"
aws_region   = "us-east-1"
```
- NEVER put `db_password` in `terraform.tfvars`. Pass it via environment variable: `export TF_VAR_db_password='...'`
- Add `terraform.tfvars` to `.gitignore` if it contains non-sensitive values you don't want in the repo. At minimum, never commit any `*.tfvars` file with secrets.

### 6. VPC module (`terraform/modules/vpc/main.tf`)
```hcl
resource "aws_vpc" "main" {
  cidr_block           = "10.0.0.0/16"
  enable_dns_support   = true
  enable_dns_hostnames = true
  tags = { Name = "${var.project_name}-vpc" }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "public" {
  count             = 2
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, count.index)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  map_public_ip_on_launch = true
  tags = { Name = "${var.project_name}-public-${count.index}" }
}

resource "aws_subnet" "private" {
  count             = 2
  vpc_id            = aws_vpc.main.id
  cidr_block        = cidrsubnet("10.0.0.0/16", 8, count.index + 10)
  availability_zone = data.aws_availability_zones.available.names[count.index]
  tags = { Name = "${var.project_name}-private-${count.index}" }
}

resource "aws_internet_gateway" "main" {
  vpc_id = aws_vpc.main.id
  tags   = { Name = "${var.project_name}-igw" }
}

data "aws_availability_zones" "available" {
  state = "available"
}
```
- Always use at least 2 AZs for high availability (databases, ECS, etc.).
- Separate public (web-facing) and private (DB, internal) subnets.

### 7. Call modules in `terraform/main.tf`
```hcl
module "vpc" {
  source       = "./modules/vpc"
  project_name = var.project_name
}

# Example: S3 bucket for app assets
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "assets" {
  bucket = "${var.project_name}-assets-${var.environment}"
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_versioning" "assets" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.assets.id
  versioning_configuration { status = "Enabled" }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block" "assets" {
  bucket = aws_s3_bucket.assets.id
  block_public_acls       = true
  ignore_public_acls      = true
  block_public_policy     = true
  restrict_public_buckets = true
}
```

### 8. Initialize and apply
```bash
cd terraform
terraform init          # downloads providers, configures backend
terraform validate      # check syntax
terraform fmt -recursive  # format all .tf files
terraform plan          # preview changes — review carefully
terraform apply         # apply after reviewing the plan
```
- Always review `terraform plan` output before applying. Destruction of resources is shown with `-` lines in red.
- Use `terraform plan -out=tfplan && terraform apply tfplan` to ensure you apply exactly what you reviewed.

### 9. GitHub Actions workflow for CI/CD
- Create `.github/workflows/terraform.yml`:
```yaml
name: Terraform

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'terraform/**'
  push:
    branches: [main]
    paths:
      - 'terraform/**'

permissions:
  id-token: write    # for OIDC
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  terraform:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    defaults:
      run:
        working-directory: terraform
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Configure AWS credentials (OIDC)
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::${{ vars.AWS_ACCOUNT_ID }}:role/GitHubActionsRole
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Set up Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: "~1.6"

      - name: Terraform Init
        run: terraform init

      - name: Terraform Validate
        run: terraform validate

      - name: Terraform Plan
        id: plan
        run: terraform plan -no-color -out=tfplan
        env:
          TF_VAR_db_password: ${{ secrets.TF_VAR_DB_PASSWORD }}

      - name: Comment plan on PR
        if: github.event_name == 'pull_request'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const output = `#### Terraform Plan
            \`\`\`\n${{ steps.plan.outputs.stdout }}\n\`\`\``;
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: output
            });

      - name: Terraform Apply
        if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push'
        run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan
        env:
          TF_VAR_db_password: ${{ secrets.TF_VAR_DB_PASSWORD }}
```
- Use OIDC (`id-token: write`) instead of long-lived AWS access keys. It is significantly more secure.
- Only run `apply` on `push` to `main`, not on PRs.

### 10. Add to .gitignore
```
# Terraform
terraform/.terraform/
terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl  # commit this file to pin provider versions
terraform/*.tfstate
terraform/*.tfstate.backup
terraform/tfplan
terraform/crash.log
terraform/override.tf
```
- Note: `.terraform.lock.hcl` should actually be committed. Remove it from `.gitignore` if it was added.

### Common errors & fixes
- **`Error: Backend configuration changed`** → Run `terraform init -reconfigure` after changing the backend.
- **`Error: Failed to get existing workspaces: S3 bucket does not exist`** → The state bucket doesn't exist yet. Bootstrap it manually (step 3) before running `terraform init`.
- **`Error acquiring the state lock`** → A previous run crashed. Check DynamoDB for a lock record and delete it if the run is confirmed finished: `aws dynamodb delete-item --table-name ... --key '{"LockID":{"S":"..."}}'`
- **`Error: Cycle`** → Circular resource dependency. Use `depends_on` to break cycles or restructure resource references.
- **`terraform plan` shows unexpected replacements** → A resource property forces replacement (shown as `forces replacement`). Review carefully — this destroys and recreates the resource. For databases, this means data loss.
- **`AccessDenied` on `terraform apply`** → The IAM role/user lacks permissions for the resource being created. Add the required IAM policy.
- **Resources drift out of sync** → Another process changed infrastructure outside Terraform. Run `terraform refresh` to sync state, then `terraform plan` to reconcile.

### What NOT to do
- Do NOT store Terraform state locally (default) for team projects — always use a remote backend (S3).
- Do NOT commit `terraform.tfstate` or `terraform.tfstate.backup` to version control — they contain sensitive values in plaintext.
- Do NOT run `terraform apply` directly in CI without reviewing a `terraform plan` first.
- Do NOT use hardcoded AWS account IDs, region strings, or ARNs throughout the code — put them in variables.
- Do NOT store database passwords or secrets in `.tfvars` files committed to the repo — use environment variables (`TF_VAR_*`) or AWS Secrets Manager.
- Do NOT skip the DynamoDB lock table — without it, concurrent applies will corrupt the state file.
- Do NOT use `terraform destroy` in production without a backup and team approval.
- Do NOT ignore `terraform plan` output that shows resource replacements — a replacement on RDS means data loss.
